sentences.text: Yeah.
That, that particular area was where the German headquarters was.
So it was a dangerous area.
But I was getting a little bit apprehensive, and I didn't want to wait to get back too late because then I would-- you know, it'd be more-- could stick out.
I figured if I go back early when people are going from church, then, you know, I have a better chance of, you know, getting through.
So I decided to get down earlier and sneak out.
I snuck out of the-- of that-- it was like a big orchard.
And I started walking where the sidewalk towards the main street, that center where they-- that time they collected all the Jews in the beginning.
And as I approach the street, and I was going to cross the street, there was a horse and wagon coming from the left side.
And suddenly I hear somebody yelling, Jews!
Jews!
And so I acted automatically.
I didn't know what was going on, whether it meant me or not.
And I backed off.
I started like walking backwards.
And there was a man standing right next to me who didn't look familiar, so he could have been like a detective or somebody.
So I couldn't run.
So I turned around.
I started walking, like, slowly, nonchalantly.
And then when I came to an area between two houses where they had like a little, little alleyway, I got into that alleyway, and I started running fast.
And, on, on the other end of the alleyway was like the width of a street, you know, a small street.
There was a little, little door, a gate, so-- which was closed.
So I opened up the gate, and I want to get out.
And a boy grabs me by the neck.
He says, I got you, in Ukrainian.
And, and I knew the boy.
I knew him from school.
He was older than me, a few years older.
And there was like a-- an automatic reaction.
My hand went into my pocket.
And I said to him, you letting me go or not?
And then he says, you know I didn't mean it.
I said, I gotta run fast because I gotta meet somebody.
Now, I'll never know why he did it-- whether he changed his mind or whether he thought I had something in my pocket-- and, and but whatever it was, I-- I don't-- really don't know.
So I got through him, and I started running.
Now, meanwhile, the other guys were running from, from the other side.
And it was a matter of-- if he would have held me there for 30 seconds, then the other guys would have been there already, and they would have gotten me.
It turns out that the other guy was my grandfather's next door neighbor's son, Polish, Polish boy, Olek Criefski.
And so I'm running and crossing the street in a different spot, and they're coming from behind.
And I ran as fast as I could.
And I remember having my mind made up that if they're behind me when I get close to the house, I'm not going to go in because then, you know, they'll, they'll get everybody.
So and I just made it to the house.
I got in.
And I got into the hiding place.
And then I heard them outside running, running right next to the house and looking, trying to find out where I got in.
And--
This was a boy-- next door neighbor of my grandparents' house, you know.
And I knew him.
He knew me.
And I remember being in his house once.
Back to the old hiding place where that teacher was--
--With other people.
Yeah.
So now we stayed there a little bit longer, not too much longer.
And then one morning-- we, we had a window in that-- in that room also that was blocked up.
I sort of peeked out, and I saw a Ukrainian man dr-- dressed in civilian clothes.
And one of them also was like a neighbor of my grandfather's.
But he worked for the fire department.
And they were going around with hatchets, and they were closing off the Jewish homes that were now abandoned.
Nobody lived there anymore.
So they were working on, on the-- they were working on the house across the street, and they were nailing it shut.
First, they went in.
They took out whatever, you know.
And there were people around there, some of the farmers trying to get something.
And then when they finished with that house, our house, the one I was in, came next.
So they came inside, and they were looking and, and now, that particular room that we were in was like at the-- at the end of the house in a very narrow room.
And there was a door going into that room that they, they closed off.
And they put a closet like this clock next to it.
They put a closet that blocked off the door.
And there was no entrance to that room, which gave the appearance that that was already the other side of the house because that particular house was a long house, and two families shared it.
And there was a wall between the two.
So that's why they probably didn't find it for such a long time because they knew how to look for hiding places, but this looks like it's a wall.
So they went to the other side of the house, and they missed it.
And one of them got suspicious and pushed away the closet.
And he said, I thought there was a door here maybe.
And he took the hatchet and hit it into the wood.
And there was another house right ne-- right next to this house.
And there was a tiny alleyway between the two houses.
And there was a window going up from that room to the-- between the two houses.
And for some reason, I had my eye on that window.
I said, in case something happens, I'll try to get out of that window.
And when I saw the light-- the minute I saw the hatchet coming through, the first light-- we were ly-- basically lying on a bed there.
There was nothing else to do all day.
So I had pants on and a-- and a shirt.
But I did not have shoes on.
And I went for the window just the way I was.
And I had some things with me that I didn't manage to take, even.
And I got out of that window.
And I made my way to the front.
And I got out between the little alleyway between the two houses.
And I see there are people there.
And suddenly I hear them saying, Jews, Jews, Jews.
So I figured they mean me.
And so I'm waiting for somebody to grab me, and nobody's grabbing me, so I keep walking away.
And they meant the other Jews that they, they captured.
So at that point, they were four-- there were five people in there besides me.
And so they, they captured all of them.
And the, the people that were around there, they were waiting for them to take away the, the Jews out of the house so they can go in and grab whatever's left, basically.
That was a mentality cause in that part of the world, a pair of pants, a jacket, or a coat and shoes, you know, it's very important to them.
So then I made my way-- I got like on the main street on the sidewalk, and I kept going towards leaving the town.
And I passed this big mill that was in town.
And I, I kept going to, to the first village that where-- that I was familiar with because there was a Ukrainian, an old farmer that used to be friendly with my grandparents.
So I figured I'll go there and find out what to do next.
Now, were you taking this whole trip barefooted?
Yeah.
That was the least of the problems, I can assure you.
And a lot of people there, you know, were going barefooted, which, you know-- which was OK.
Otherwise, if you walk barefooted someplace else, you get s-- you know, somebody gets suspicious.
That was in, in August.
It was still warm.
At night, it was getting cold already.
You know, in that part of the w-- country, you know, it gets cold.
I came to that man's farm.
His name was Toynka.
And, and I was hiding in the bushes, and I was waiting, you know, for a chance to either see him or to see what's happening there.
And then when I saw him, I, I went out, and I, I called him.
And he saw me, and he told me to go back, hide in the bushes.
And then he went into the house, and he was thinking it over.
At one point, one of my aunts, the youngest one, was hiding on that, that farm.
And then he came back and told me that the best thing for me to do is to go to Lubon where they still-- the Jews are still living there in a ghetto.
Yeah, like within, within a half an hour, you know.
And, and he told me which way to go-- not to go through the town, but go through the side roads, you know.
And so I started going there.
And it took me probably most of the day to get to Lubon.
I stopped in a few-- one village, you know, to ask for some food.
And then a farmer picked me up at some point, and he gave me a piece of bread.
And he gave me a piece of pork, which of course we didn't eat at that point.
And, and I took the bread, and I was eating it, but I couldn't, couldn't get the pork close to my mouth.
Then I came to Lubon.
On the way, one, one Ukrainian family wanted to convert me.
They said I could stay there, maybe work for them at the-- and they took me to someplace.
And then I got frightened.
I said, oh, I got to leave.
And I came to Lubon.
Was about close to 6:00.
And I spotted a woman on the street with a-- with a wide band with the star of David, so I knew she was Jewish.
And I came over, and I asked her-- and I lost my orientation in the town.
I was there before, before the war because my, my uncles had stores there and businesses.
And my grandfather had like four, four brothers there and two sisters, big family.
But, but I lost my orientation, and I wanted to get to a particular uncle.
His name was Falek.
So I asked this woman if he-- if she can show me where Falek Ginzburg lives.
And she said, who are you?
And I explained to her.
She happened to be his daughter-in-law.
But his son married her after I knew-- you know, I wasn't there since then.
So she went and got her brother, and the brother-- she said, we have to do it fast, because the curfew starts 6:00.
So he took me-- he took me there.
And there were a lot of people in that house.
My uncle had a big house.
It had two main floors and then like a little attic that was also fixed up like a, like, you know, a living.
Was this right in the town of Lubon?
That was in the town, the center of the town.
So being that it was a bigger house, and this was part of a ghetto, they made a lot of people move in there.
So it was crowded.
And they got a bed for me, like in the living room, set up a bed.
And, and they gave me a jacket to wear and some shoes.
And the next day, my brother showed up.
And he told me that he was captured, and he was taken to the synagogue.
And at one point, when they had enough people in there, and they were going to take them out to take them to be shot, he and another boy got into an oven that was heating the place.
And he actually crawled up to the chimney.
And the other boy was inside the oven.
And then the Ukrainian policemen went to search before, you know, when they took everybody out.
And he spotted somebody there.
He shot into the oven, and he killed the other boy.
And they wounded my brother on the side of the-- of the foot.
Then in the middle of the night somehow, he managed to get out.
And he made his way to, to Lubon.
So we slept together, you know, in, in the same bed.
And he felt guilty that he got out of that hiding place before they were trying to run away, you know?
And he said to me, I'll never, never leave you again.
Then in a few days, the same thing started happening in that town.
And it must have been like 4:00 in the morning.
I heard shots outside.
And right away, I ran to a window and looked out.
And I saw Germans and Ukrainians with dogs and with sticks.
And they were dragging people out of homes, and they were chasing them to, again, to like a, a gathering point.
One of them was a synagogue also, and then they had another gathering point.
So we knew what was happening.
And they had some hiding places prepared, so we, we, we ran down to a hiding place.
We stayed in that hiding place maybe a day.
And there was no light coming into that place, so we didn't know whether it was day or night.
At one point, one guy wanted to go out and get some water.
So he started opening up the opening.
The opening was like part of a wall that moved, and it was underneath an oven.
And as he was opening it up, there were two Ukrainian policemen in the room.
And one of them right away shot into the wall.
And the bullet was right next to me.
So and they-- you know, the policemen already knew that there were people there.
And they tried to close the opening.
They figured they'll close the opening again.
And to me, that was stupid.
I said, why close the opening?
They know already where we are.
Let's get out.
So I started crawling out.
And the entrance was like on the bottom, and you had to go out and to the right.
And there was a hole like to get out.
When I came to the opening, the policeman pushed me back, didn't let me-- didn't let me out.
And then I tried it again.
And he-- the policeman said I should collect-- tell them to give us gold.
So I went back inside, and I told them, you know, there are two policemen there, and they want some gold.
Maybe if we give them the gold, maybe they'll go away.
And the people were hesitating.
And then finally, they took a hat, and they put in rings and watches and whatnot.
And I brought it to the opening.
And meanwhile, you know, and I gave it to the guy, and I crawled out.
And I pulled my brother, who was more like frightened.
He was petrified.
He was afraid to get out.
So I was actually pulling him by the hair.
And then he came out, too.
And while they were dividing up the gold and stuff, putting it in their pockets, there was a window right next to that hiding place with wood like for burning next to the window.
And, and if you got on top of the window, there was like a hallway with steps going upstairs.
So I got on top of the wood.
And, and then I got my brother also, got on top.
And then we, we, we got on the window.
And then suddenly, the Ukrainian caught on to what, you know, we were doing and then grabbed his rifle and cocked it and said, come on down, or I'll shoot.
So we had to come down.
And then one of the Ukrainians says to me, tell him to give us karbovantsiv.
The karbovantsiv was-- during that period of time, the Ukrainians thought that they were going to have an independent Ukraine, so they had their own money.
They were called karbovantsiv.
So he wanted to get-- I should get money.
So I crawled in, and I asked them.
I said, do you want-- they want the karbovantsiv.
So they got together a bunch of money.
And I gave it to them again.
And he saw all that money.
You know, they started dividing.
I made for the window again with my brother.
We took off our shoes and ran up the steps.
And there were a lot of rooms there, so we went to one of the rooms.
And, like, we hid under a bed.
And then one of them realized that we, we, we took off.
So he ran up the steps, and he ran to the rooms and started looking for us.
He actually looked into that room, but didn't see anything.
And then the other guy was calling him to come down.
And so he went down.
And then when, when it was quiet, we made it to a window.
This was, like, one floor up.
So we managed to get out of the window to the roof of another building.
And there was like-- the roof was already ripped open.
People, like, wanted to be able to move from one place to the other.
So there was a hole in the roof.
Some shingles were broken.
So we got in there.
And then there was, like, an opening to a ladder that went down to the room.
There was a house.
And so we went down to the house, not really knowing what, what we were doing.
We were just looking for a place.
And that house was already also, like, robbed.
The curtains were off the windows, and most of the things were gone.
And just about that time-- through the window, we could see the synagogue.
There was, like, a big synagogue.
And this, this militia man was going with a rifle towards the synagogue.
And he managed-- he happened to turn towards that window, where we were in that-- in that room that we were in.
And, you know, you think you're inside.
Nobody sees you from the outside.
And he spotted somebody inside, and he started walking towards the house.
So we realized that he's coming in that direction, so we ran back up to the attic on that ladder and closed the door.
He came into the house.
And he started climbing up the ladder.
And both of-- my brother and I were sitting on the opening.
And he came up, and he tried to push the door open.
And we were holding as much as we could.
The ladder was very tall.
There was, like, not such a strong setup, so he couldn't apply too much force.
He would have fallen off.
And so he saw it doesn't open.
He went down and went away.
Then we sort of stayed in that attic for a while.
We didn't know if there were other people there or not.
And then I heard-- when they took all the people out of that hiding place where I was, and there was my father's first cousin there, Kalman, who had the same name as my father.
And he was like an invalid.
I think he got hit with polio or something or, you know, so his father supported him.
They lived in an apartment there.
So it was his wife-- he, his wife, and two children.
And there were a lot of other people there, but they were there.
And they were taking him out.
And the little girl was asking, sort of in a crying voice-- people were crying --asking her mother in Yiddish, mother, mother, are they going to shoot us?
And I don't remember exactly what the mother said to her.
And then they took him away.
And that night, we decided to go down to one of the hiding places to look for food.
And my brother wouldn't let me go.
He said, no, you stay here.
I'm going to go.
And then he went.
And I figured if he can go and find food, I can also.
I, you know, I was in this other place.
I know where it is.
So I went down to that old-- to the hiding place where we got caught, except somebody took away all the wood, and it was difficult already to, to climb.
And I went down there.
There was no bread.
There was some other stuff.
My uncle had a factory, a soda, soda factory.
So I found some, like, bottles of the syrups I took.
And then suddenly, I heard German voices yelling and screaming.
And they captured-- they captured the attic.
By that time, my brother probably was back in the attic or still in the house.
And I never-- I never saw him again after that.
And one of our cousins found us before in the attic, and she told us that in the middle of the night, we'll be-- they'll be leaving town and where to meet them.
So I, I had trouble getting out of that hiding place, but I managed to get out.
And I went to that place where my cousin told us to meet him.
And I met him there.
And then they started getting-- it was, like, in the middle of the night by then.
And it was pitch dark.
It was, like, October.
They started the 1st.
It was maybe October 2nd or 3rd.
And we started getting out of the hou-- of the town.
I had a problem.
I didn't know the town that much.
And I had to hold on to somebody because if I got disconnected, I wouldn't know where to go.
And then we made it-- made it out of town.
There was a railroad crossing there.
We had trouble getting through because the Germans were shooting off rockets to light up the place.
No, no, no.
These were all my cousins.
Actually, they were my father's first cousins.
So one of them was about 19, one, 20.
There was one boy with the group that came out of town.
There were six total of us.
And one of them was about 14.
No, no, no.
You just-- you went with the stream, and if you could make it, you went.
If you didn't-- you know.
There was no, no protection.
In fact, at one point, we were walking a lot, and we sat down near a cemeter-- near a cemetery to rest, and I fell asleep.
And then when I woke up, everybody was gone.
And I was looking for them in the cemetery.
I couldn't find them.
So I went someplace else, to a farm.
And I was hiding in an attic on the farm.
In the morning, the woman came in to milk the cow, so I made a noise, and she got scared, you know.
But she saw me, and she gave me some food.
And she said, after the boys take out the cows, you can-- you can leave.
So now you were on your own again?
So now-- then I somehow find these people in the cemetery.
Outside of the cemetery, they were hiding.
And so we-- I joined them again.
And we went to another village and hiding in the woods.
And then one night, we were going towards-- in the direction of [NON-ENGLISH] because we knew the Jews were still there in the ghetto.
And in the middle of the night, we were on the road, and we heard a horse and wagon approaching.
So we got off the road and waited till that passed.
I was-- I was sort of tired, so I was lagging behind.
I was walking a little bit, maybe 50 feet or so, 25 feet, behind them.
And everybody went off the road and were lying in a ditch.
So at that particular point, when the-- when the horse and wagon passed, I heard something that sounded like making noise like a bicycle.
And then I saw this guy pass me.
And he stopped ahead of me, took out his rifle, and shot in the air and started yelling for everybody to come out.
So somehow, somebody told that there were six people walking there, you know, and alerted the Ukrainian militia, and they came.
And pretty soon more, more of them came.
Now, when I saw what was happening, I started crawling away on the grass, like in the ditch.
And then more police came with the flashlights, and they started shining.
And, and they were concentrating on the main body where the other five people were.
And, and-- but then they noticed that I was missing, a sixth one.
They knew there were six.
The sixth one was missing.
They were yelling, where's the sixth one?
And then they, they were shooting.
They spotted me running.
And they were shooting in that direction.
And then I, I spotted a, a drain pipe going underneath the road.
And I crawled into that drain pipe.
